title: Understanding the Linux File System
description: Learn how the Linux file system is organized — where your files go, what each folder does, and why there's no C: drive.

steps.title: Forget drive letters
steps.content: On Windows, you have `C:\`, `D:\`, etc. Linux doesn't use drive letters. Instead, everything starts from a single root directory: `/`. All files, folders, devices, and even other drives live under this one tree. Your USB drive doesn't become `E:` — it gets "mounted" at a path like `/media/your-name/usb-drive/`.

steps.title: Your home directory: /home
steps.content: This is **your** space. Documents, Downloads, Music, Desktop — all your personal files live here. It's like `C:\Users\YourName` on Windows. Each user gets their own folder.

steps.title: System directories you should know
steps.content:
Here are the most important top-level directories:

- `/` — Root, the top of everything
- `/home` — Your personal files
- `/etc` — System configuration files
- `/var` — Variable data (logs, databases, caches)
- `/tmp` — Temporary files (cleared on reboot)
- `/usr` — User programs and libraries
- `/opt` — Optional/third-party software
- `/bin` and `/sbin` — Essential system commands
- `/dev` — Hardware devices (disks, USB, etc.)
- `/mnt` and `/media` — Mounted drives and USB sticks

steps.title: Hidden files start with a dot
steps.content: Linux uses a dot prefix instead of a hidden attribute. Files and folders starting with `.` are hidden by default. Most app configs live in your home directory as hidden folders like `~/.config/`.
steps.code:
# Show hidden files:
ls -a ~/

# Common hidden config folders:
ls ~/.config/
ls ~/.local/share/

steps.title: Paths use forward slashes
steps.content: Windows uses backslashes (`C:\Users\Documents`), Linux uses forward slashes (`/home/user/Documents`). Paths are also **case-sensitive** — `Documents` and `documents` are two different folders.

steps.title: See disk usage and mounted drives
steps.content: Check how much space you have, and see where drives are mounted.
steps.code:
# Disk usage summary:
df -h

# Size of a specific folder:
du -sh ~/Downloads

# List mounted drives:
lsblk

seo.title: Linux File System Explained for Beginners
seo.description: Understand how the Linux file system works. Learn what /home, /etc, /var, and other directories do — explained for Windows and macOS switchers.
